lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying medical diagnostic and therapeutic, industrial, research, and scientific irradiation apparatus, tubes, components, upgrades, and associated configured systems to external healthcare, laboratory, industrial, OEM, and research customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospitals and imaging-service operators, radioactive-isotope producers, captive internal engineering units, software-only vendors without accountable physical apparatus, pure distributors, non-control financing vehicles, and operations that cannot transfer independently of the owner.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Capital-equipment, component, tube, upgrade, replacement, and configured-system revenue under purchase orders, tenders, OEM programs, service-linked installed-base relationships, and regulated product lifecycles.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation structure for broader NAICS 334500 instrument manufacturing, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in 334517. In May 2023, engineering occupations were 17.58%, computer/mathematical 14.10%, business/financial 10.15%, management 12.47%, office support 7.77%, and production 26.89%. I assign meaningful exposure to requirements review, software and test documentation, regulatory submissions, technical writing, configuration, procurement, planning, service knowledge, and administration, but low exposure to hardware assembly, calibration, radiation testing, installation, physical verification, and accountable safety decisions. Irradiation manufacturers may carry more regulatory and systems-engineering labor than the broader sector, increasing assistable work while limiting substitution.
a.rationale: The industry is engineering- and documentation-intensive, giving AI a larger addressable knowledge-work layer than most manufacturing. Safety-critical design, physics, validation, calibration, assembly, and regulatory accountability remain human-led and physical.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study from 8-12 independent irradiation-equipment firms, separated into hardware, software, systems engineering, regulatory, quality, production, field service, commercial, and administration and documenting current AI use, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Occupation evidence covers all navigational, measuring, electromedical, and control instruments, not 334517.
Exposure does not imply autonomous engineering or regulatory approval.

rho.rationale: AI assistance in requirements traceability, test-document drafting, code review, service knowledge, procurement, planning, and regulatory document assembly is feasible within five years. Design controls, verification and validation, cybersecurity, quality systems, radiation performance standards, customer qualification, auditability, and the need for accountable expert sign-off constrain operational implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Validated deployments reporting sustained engineering and quality hours, requirement defects, software-test coverage, document deviations, audit findings, service resolution, false outputs, and regulator or customer acceptance would materially update the range.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed implementation probability.
Medical requirements do not apply identically to industrial and research apparatus.

e.rationale: Many independent manufacturers repeatedly supply OEMs or end users and have transferable designs, certifications, staff, installed-base relationships, and quality systems. Large strategic subsidiaries, captive units, product companies outside the true EBITDA band, pre-revenue ventures, single-product regulatory dependence, and founder-centric technical or customer relationships reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A verified list of the 45 dataset-estimated candidates with ownership, product and customer recurrence, normalized EBITDA, regulatory status, installed base, customer concentration, key-person dependence, and standalone quality systems would replace this estimate.
caveats: The supplied n relies on receipts and a computers/peripherals margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
The industry includes medical, industrial, and research products with different qualification and revenue patterns.

s5.rationale: Strategic medtech and industrial buyers value technology, regulatory clearances, installed bases, and service access, while founders and sponsors can create sales. A small universe, long technical and regulatory diligence, product liability, customer concentration, key-person risk, and internal strategic ownership reduce qualifying transfer frequency.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched ten-year transaction set from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Medtech Insight, and specialist advisers, paired with owner-age, sponsor-hold, and succession data for eligible firms, would materially change the probability.
caveats: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
Technology licenses, product-line asset sales, and internal reorganizations must be excluded unless operating control transfers.

q.rationale: Regulatory clearance, safety performance, installed interfaces, uptime, service knowledge, training, and validation create switching costs and differentiated value. Hospital tenders, GPOs, OEM procurement, public research bids, reimbursement pressure, and long capital cycles still pass some productivity gains into price or bundled service.
q.change_evidence: Customer and OEM contracts, tender histories, GPO terms, price realization, service attach and renewal rates, upgrade pricing, cost-disclosure clauses, and margin bridges after workflow improvements would establish actual retention.
caveats: No source directly measures sharing of AI-derived benefit.
Pricing power differs sharply between proprietary cleared systems, OEM components, and competitive research apparatus.

d5.rationale: Imaging, radiation therapy, security and inspection, scientific research, component replacement, upgrades, and installed-base maintenance support physical demand. Hospital capital budgets, reimbursement, equipment longevity, consolidation, refurbished units, alternative modalities, and research cycles create downside, while aging equipment and technological upgrades support growth.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census shipments by modality, FDA clearance and installation data, hospital capital plans, CMS utilization, installed-base age, tube replacement volumes, industrial inspection orders, and research funding forecasts would materially change the range.
caveats: No complete constant-price five-year forecast was found for 334517.
Medical imaging dominates available evidence but the NAICS definition also includes therapy, industrial, research, and scientific apparatus.

o.rationale: Remaining demand is for physical radiation generation, detection, control, shielding, and configured systems and requires accountable design, manufacture, calibration, safety testing, documentation, installation, and release. AI may improve image analysis or workflows but cannot replace the apparatus; refurbishment, vertical integration, or imports shift the operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing interviews and lost-order data quantifying refurbished-system substitution, OEM insourcing, imports, alternative non-ionizing modalities, remote software substitution, and component standardization would materially change this value.
caveats: Software may change clinical or industrial workflows without eliminating the physical radiation source.
Domestic suppliers can be displaced even when US end demand persists.

narrative.executive_view: Irradiation apparatus combines a large engineering, software, quality, and regulatory labor layer with physical safety-critical hardware. AI can improve traceability, documentation, testing support, service knowledge, and planning, but regulation, validation, and expert accountability constrain implementation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist requirements and revision review, test-case generation, code review, risk and regulatory document assembly, complaint and service knowledge, procurement, configuration, and planning. Physics design, hardware integration, assembly, calibration, radiation measurement, verification, installation, and final safety release remain expert and physical.
narrative.value_capture: Clearances, performance standards, installed interfaces, uptime, training, and service history protect value. OEM sourcing, hospital procurement, GPOs, tenders, capital-budget pressure, and reimbursement still force some benefit into customer economics.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied dataset estimates 45 firms in the economic band. Actual eligibility depends on independence, recurring products or installed-base revenue, valid clearances and quality systems, customer concentration, key-person risk, and normalized earnings; many apparent participants may be large strategic subsidiaries.
narrative.demand_durability: Diagnostic imaging, therapy, industrial inspection, scientific research, tubes, upgrades, and service-linked replacements require physical apparatus. Equipment longevity and capital constraints temper demand, while upgrades, installed-base aging, and clinical or industrial needs support it.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest unknowns are six-digit occupation mix, medical versus industrial product mix, regulatory status, product liability, customer concentration, installed-base economics, current software automation, and true LMM margins. The supplied inputs mix vintages and use a computers/peripherals margin bridge.
narrative.principal_driver: A high-value engineering, software, regulatory, test, and service-document layer creates more AI-addressable work than typical physical manufacturing.
narrative.principal_weakness: A small candidate pool and stringent safety, validation, and regulatory requirements slow implementation and transactions.

sources.title: Navigational, Measuring, Electromedical, and Control Instruments Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS
sources.supports: BLS reports NAICS 334500 occupation shares of 17.58% engineering, 14.10% computer/mathematical, 10.15% business/financial, 12.47% management, 7.77% office support, and 26.89% production in May 2023.

sources.title: NAICS 334517 Irradiation Apparatus Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census defines the industry as manufacturing irradiation apparatus and tubes for medical diagnostic, medical therapeutic, industrial, research, and scientific evaluation using beta, gamma, X-ray, or other ionizing radiation.

sources.title: Medical X-ray Imaging
sources.supports: FDA states that X-ray manufacturers are regulated under EPRC and medical-device provisions and must meet records, defect notification, repair/replacement, import, radiation-safety performance, and medical-device requirements.

sources.title: How Does FDA Regulate CT Systems?
sources.supports: FDA states that CT systems are regulated as radiation-emitting products and Class II medical devices, requiring manufacturer registration/listing, 510(k) notification, quality-system practices, controls, certification, labeling, and performance information.
